title: STANDARD FOR KIWIFRUIT CXS 338-2020 Adopted in 2020.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR KIWIFRUIT CXS 338-2020 Adopted in 2020.
sections.summary: Document title and adoption year: 'Standard for Kiwifruit CXS 338-2020' adopted in 2020; no substantive requirements in this item.

Standard defines quality requirements for kiwifruit after preparation and packaging, allows slight lack of freshness/turgidity or slight deterioration post-packaging, and obliges the holder/seller not to display, offer, deliver or market products except in conformity with the Standard, with the holder/seller responsible for compliance.

Applies to fresh kiwifruit (Actinidia) of varieties derived from Actinidia chinensis and A. deliciosa and their hybrids supplied fresh to the consumer; kiwifruit for industrial processing are excluded.

Sets quality provisions for kiwifruit including minimum requirements (intact, sound, adequately firm, well formed, clean, practically free of pests/damage, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign odours/tastes, fresh in appearance, suitable for transport), minimum maturity thresholds, and classification rules into three classes with specific defect tolerances.

Minimum requirements for all classes: fruit must be intact (peduncle excluded), sound, adequately firm (not soft/shrivelled/water-soaked), well formed (no double/multiple fruit), clean, practically free of pests and pest damage, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign odours/tastes, fresh in appearance, and able to withstand transport and handling to arrive in satisfactory condition.
Kiwifruit must have reached an appropriate degree of maturity for the variety and attain at least 6.2° Brix or an average dry matter content of 15%.
Kiwifruit are classified into three classes (Extra, Class I and Class II), each with defined quality criteria and allowable defects described in subsequent subsections.

The 'Extra' class requires kiwifruit of superior quality characteristic of the variety with sound flesh, round/oval cross-section (minimum equatorial diameter ratio ≥ 0.8), and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I kiwifruit must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety with sound flesh and round/oval cross-section (equatorial diameter ratio ≥ 0.7); slight defects are permitted provided they do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation, including slight shape defects (no swelling/malformations), slight colouring defects, superficial skin defects up to 1 cm², and small Hayward marks without protuberance.

Class II includes kiwifruit that meet the minimum requirements but not higher class criteria; allowed defects (while retaining essential quality, keeping quality and presentation) include shape defects (including flattened fruit), colouring defects, skin defects up to 2 cm², more pronounced Hayward marks with slight protuberance, and slight bruising.

Sets sizing options for kiwifruit (by weight, count, or trade practice) and requires packages to be labelled with the size and method used; for A. chinensis, A. deliciosa and hybrids minimum weights are Extra 90 g, Class I 70 g, Class II 65 g, and within-weight uniformity tolerances are prescribed as 10 g (≤85 g), 15 g (>85–120 g), 20 g (>120–150 g) and 40 g (>150 g).

Allows specified quality tolerances per lot and re-sorting to achieve conformity, with class-specific allowances: Extra class up to 5% not meeting Extra but meeting Class I (including 0.5% decay/soft rot/internal breakdown applying only beyond the export control point), Class I up to 10% not meeting Class I but meeting Class II (including 1% decay/soft rot/internal breakdown), and Class II up to 10% failing class and minimum requirements (including up to 2% affected by decay/soft rot/internal breakdown); size tolerances for all classes (if sized) are 10% by number or weight.

Class II: up to 10% by number or weight of kiwifruit may fail to meet the class or minimum requirements, and of that amount no more than 2% in total may be affected by decay, soft rot or internal breakdown.
For all classes, when kiwifruit are sized, up to 10% by number or weight may deviate from the sizing requirements.

Requires packaged kiwifruit to be uniform in origin, variety, quality and size (though distinct varieties may be mixed if uniform in quality and origin), mandates that the visible contents represent the whole, and prescribes packaging materials and container characteristics to protect produce and avoid damage, with compliance to the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport (CXC 44-1995) and prohibition of foreign matter or odours.

Each package must contain kiwifruit uniform in origin, variety (cultivar), quality and size, though distinctly different varieties may be packed together if they are uniform in quality and origin, and the visible contents must represent the entire package.

Specifies packaging requirements for kiwifruit: materials inside packages must be food-grade, clean and non-damaging; printed materials must use non-toxic inks/adhesives; stickers must not leave visible glue or cause skin defects; and packing must comply with CXC 44-1995.
Containers must provide appropriate quality, hygiene, ventilation and strength for safe handling, shipping and preservation of kiwifruit, and packages must be free of foreign matter and odours.

Sets labelling/marking requirements for kiwifruit: consumer and non-retail packages must meet the General Standard for Labelling plus specific provisions on name, origin and particulars, with non-retail packages to carry legible, indelible particulars visible from outside and provisions for bulk transport documentation or machine-readable electronic alternatives.

States that consumer packages must comply with the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) and additional kiwifruit-specific labelling provisions set out in this standard.
Requires each package to be labelled with the name of the produce and optionally the variety(ies), cultivar(s) or predominant flesh colour.
Requires declaration of the country of origin and optionally the district, national, regional or local place name on consumer packages.

Non-retail packages must bear legible, indelible particulars grouped on the same side and visible externally, and for bulk transport these particulars must appear on accompanying documents or as machine-readable electronic identification accessible inside the transport vehicle.
Non-retail packages must show the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher, with an optional identification code.

Non-retail packages must state the name of the produce and may include variety or cultivar names or predominant flesh colour; a variety name may be replaced by a synonym, and a trade name may only be used in addition to the variety or synonym.
Non-retail packages must indicate the country of origin and may optionally include the district or national, regional or local place name.

Commercial specifications required on non-retail packages include class and, if sized, the size expressed either by minimum and maximum fruit weight, by number of fruit and net fruit weight, or by the size and method used.
Heading only: mentions an optional official inspection mark but contains no substantive requirements.
Requires kiwifruit to comply with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides and with maximum levels set in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995).

Recommends handling and preparation of kiwifruit in accordance with Codex hygienic texts (General Principles of Food Hygiene CXC 1-1969, Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables CXC 53-2003, etc.) and that produce comply with any established microbiological criteria per CXG 21-1997.
